# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## What This Directory Is

RISC-V architecture-specific math library (`libm`) optimizations for picolibc. These replace generic C implementations from `libm/common/` and `libm/math/` with hand-tuned assembly or hardware-FPU-aware C code.

See `README.md` in this directory for detailed function listings and build configuration matrix.

## Build System

This directory is built as part of the top-level picolibc Meson build. There is no standalone build. The `meson.build` here populates `srcs_libm_machine` which gets compiled into the libm static library.

A separate `CMakeLists.txt` exists for CMake-based builds but only includes a minimal subset (C files, no assembly, no configuration branching).

### Key build flags (set in top-level `meson.build`, consumed here)

- `is_softfp` — software floating-point ABI (no hardware FPU)
- `is_double_abi` — hardware double-precision FPU
- `is_riscv64` — 64-bit RISC-V
- `is_rvm` — M-extension (hardware multiply/divide)
- `is_rv32e` — RV32E reduced 16-register ISA

### Per-target library quirk

For soft-float RV32 configs (both standard and RV32E), `sf_sincos.c` is compiled as a separate per-target `static_library` via the `foreach params : targets` loop, not added to `srcs_libm_machine`. This is because it needs per-multilib `c_args`. The resulting `lib_machine<target>` libraries are linked via `link_whole` elsewhere.

## Assembly Conventions

The `.s` files were originally written for the Andes NDS32 ISA and transliterated to RISC-V. Comments preserve the original NDS32 mnemonics (e.g., `# push $lp`, `# swi $r0, [$sp + #4]`).

### Register mapping pattern

Assembly uses standard RISC-V register names (`x10`–`x20`, `sp`, `ra`, `t6`). The NDS32-to-RISC-V register mapping is:
- `$r0`–`$r5` → `x10`–`x15` (argument/return registers `a0`–`a5`)
- `$r6`–`$r9` → `x9`, `x18`–`x20` (saved registers)
- `$lp` → `ra` (return address)
- `$ta` → `t6` (temporary)

### Internal call graph

Assembly math functions call shared Andes helper routines (`andes_*.s`). Key dependency chains:
- Trig functions → `__gf_dpreduct`/`__gf_fpreduct` (argument reduction) → `__gf_exmul`/`__gf_exadd` (extended precision) → polynomial evaluators (`__gf_p4`–`__gf_p8`)
- `exp`/`log`/`pow` → `__gf_dpexexp`/`__gf_dpexlog` → extended precision helpers
- All assembly shares coefficient tables: `__gf_dpsincon`, `__gf_dpcoscon`, `__gf_fpsincof`, `__gf_fpcoscof`, etc.

### RV32E variants

`rv32e/` contains the same routines rewritten to use only `x0`–`x15` (16 registers). When modifying an assembly routine, the corresponding `rv32e/` version must also be updated.

## Hard-float C Code (`hardfloat/`)

Optimized C implementations that exploit hardware FPU instructions. Originally from ARM's Optimized Routines project (Apache 2.0 licensed).

- Uses `#pragma GCC optimize ("O3")` for aggressive optimization
- `ref_rredf.c`/`ref_rredf.h` provides the shared float argument reduction helper (`ref__mathlib_rredf`)
- Polynomial coefficients are stored in global arrays (not `static const`) to control memory layout for performance

## Standalone Test Builds (outside Meson)

For quick accuracy/correctness testing of individual assembly routines, use the standalone `Makefile` in this directory. It compiles test C files and links them against the relevant assembly objects directly.

### Toolchain

Use the Andes `nds32le-elf-mculib-v5` toolchain for RV32 soft-float builds:

```
/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5/bin/riscv32-elf-gcc
```

- GCC 14.2.0, bare-metal (`riscv32-elf`)
- Ships with newlib (provides printf, math reference functions, etc.)
- `-mvh` flag enables Andes virtual hosting (semihosting) for I/O via QEMU/GDB
- Target flags: `-march=rv32im -mabi=ilp32` for soft-float M-extension builds

### QEMU

Use the Andes QEMU v5 build for running RV32 test ELFs:

```
/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
```

- QEMU 9.2.0 (Andes fork, v9.2.0-401-g4581fc99a4)
- Run a semihosting test ELF:
  ```sh
  /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32 \
      -cpu rv32 -M virt -nographic -bios none \
      -semihosting -kernel test_sinf.elf
  ```

### Example: build and run sinf test

```sh
make clean && make          # builds test_sinf.elf
/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32 -cpu andes-n25  -nographic   -M andes_ae350  -semihosting -bios test_sinf.elf
```

## Naming Conventions

- `e_` — IEEE error-handling wrapper functions (e.g., `e_sqrt` wraps the core sqrt)
- `s_` — double-precision standard functions
- `sf_` — single-precision (float) standard functions
- `k_` — kernel helpers (internal, e.g., `k_sin`, `k_cos`)
- `er_` — reentrant variants (e.g., `er_lgamma`)
- `andes_` — shared assembly building blocks (not standalone math functions)

# RISC-V libm Machine-Specific Optimizations

This directory contains architecture-specific math library optimizations for
RISC-V targets. The build system selects one of four configurations based on
compile-time detection of hardware capabilities.

## Build Configurations

The following boolean flags (detected in the top-level `meson.build`) control
which sources are compiled:

| Flag            | Meaning                                          |
|-----------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `is_softfp`    | No hardware FPU (software floating-point ABI)    |
| `is_double_abi`| Hardware double-precision FPU ABI                |
| `is_riscv64`   | 64-bit RISC-V (`riscv64` CPU family)             |
| `is_rvm`       | M-extension present (hardware multiply/divide)   |
| `is_rv32e`     | RV32E base ISA (reduced 16-register file)        |

These combine into four target configurations:

### 1. Hard-float (`!is_softfp`)

Targets with a hardware FPU. Optimized C implementations live in `hardfloat/`.

**Single-precision (float) optimizations:**
- `sf_atan`, `sf_log`, `sf_exp`, `sf_pow`, `sf_fabs`
- `sf_sin`, `sf_cos`, `sf_sincos`

**Double-precision optimizations (requires `is_double_abi`):**
- `s_fabs`, `e_rem_pio2`, `s_sin`, `s_cos`, `s_atan`, `k_sin`, `k_cos`, `s_sincos`

**Common utilities (both float and double):**
- `s_fmax`, `s_fmin`, `s_fpclassify`, `s_lrint`, `s_lround`, `s_sqrt`
- `sf_fpclassify`, `sf_sqrt`
- `ref_rredf` (float argument reduction helper)

### 2. Soft-float RV32 + M-extension (`is_softfp && !is_riscv64 && is_rvm && !is_rv32e`)

The most heavily optimized path. Nearly all math functions are replaced with
hand-written RISC-V assembly (`.s` files) using the full 32-register file and
hardware multiply/divide instructions.

**Double-precision assembly routines:**
- Trigonometric: `sin`, `cos`, `tan`, `asin`, `acos`, `atan`, `atan2`, `sincos` (via helpers)
- Hyperbolic: `sinh`, `cosh`, `tanh`, `asinh`, `acosh` (via `e_acosh`), `atanh` (via `e_atanh`)
- Exponential/logarithmic: `exp`, `expm1`, `log`, `log1p`, `log10`, `pow`
- Bessel: `j0`, `j1`, `jn`, `y0`, `y1`, `yn`
- Error functions: `erf`, `erfc`, `lgamma`
- Root/power: `sqrt`, `cbrt`, `hypot`
- Rounding: `ceil`, `floor`, `round`, `rint`, `trunc`, `lrint`, `lround`
- Remainder/modular: `fmod`, `remainder`, `remquo`, `rem_pio2`
- Classification/manipulation: `fabs`, `fmax`, `fmin`, `fpclassify`, `frexp`, `ldexp`, `ilogb`, `modf`, `nextafter`, `scalb`, `scalbn`

**Single-precision assembly routines:**
- `atan`, `ceil`, `cos`, `fabs`, `floor`, `frexp`, `ldexp`, `modf`, `sin`, `tan`, `tanh`
- `acos`, `asin`, `cosh`, `exp`, `fmod`, `log`, `log10`, `pow`, `sinh`, `sqrt`
- `fpclassify`, `sincos`, `scalbn`

**Andes helper routines (assembly building blocks):**
- Extended-precision arithmetic: `exadd`, `exsub`, `exmul`, `exdiv`, `exp`
- Polynomial evaluations: `p4`–`p8`, `p4ac`–`p8ac` (with accumulated variants)
- Unpacked polynomial: `up4`–`up8`
- Argument reduction: `fpreduct` (float), `dpreduct` (double), `r`, `r4`
- Kernel approximations: `fpexatan`, `fpexexp`, `fpexlog`, `fpexsqrt` (float), `dpexatan`, `dpexexp`, `dpexlog`, `dpexsqrt` (double)
- Coefficient tables: `fpsincof`, `fpcoscof` (float), `dpsincon`, `dpcoscon`, `dpasncon` (double)
- Constants/utilities: `0`, `1`, `n`, `sigmax`

### 3. Soft-float RV32E + M-extension (`is_softfp && !is_riscv64 && is_rvm && is_rv32e`)

Same set of optimized functions as configuration 2, but with assembly rewritten
to use only registers `x0`–`x15` (the RV32E reduced register set). All sources
live in the `rv32e/` subdirectory.

### 4. Fallback (`is_softfp && (is_riscv64 || !is_rvm)`)

Minimal C-only optimizations for soft-float 64-bit targets or targets without
the M-extension.

**Optimized functions:**
- `s_fabs`, `s_fmax`, `s_fmin`, `s_fpclassify`, `s_lrint`, `s_lround`, `s_sqrt`
- `sf_fabs`, `sf_fpclassify`, `sf_sqrt`

## Directory Structure

```
.
├── hardfloat/       # C optimizations using hardware FPU instructions
├── rv32e/           # Assembly routines for RV32E (16-register) targets
├── andes_*.s        # Andes helper routines for RV32 (32-register) targets
├── e_*.s            # IEEE double-precision assembly (RV32 soft-float)
├── s_*.s / s_*.c    # Double-precision assembly/C overrides
├── sf_*.s / sf_*.c  # Single-precision assembly/C overrides
├── k_*.s            # Kernel routines (sin, cos, tan, rem_pio2)
├── copysignl.c      # Long double copysign
└── meson.build      # Build configuration
```

## Naming Conventions

- `e_` prefix: IEEE-required functions (error-handling wrappers)
- `s_` prefix: Standard double-precision functions
- `sf_` prefix: Standard single-precision (float) functions
- `k_` prefix: Kernel helper routines (internal use)
- `er_` prefix: Reentrant error functions (e.g., `er_lgamma`)
- `andes_` prefix: Andes-specific assembly helper routines

# Makefile for sinf accuracy test — A/B comparison
# Target: RV32IM soft-float (Andes optimized assembly)
#
# Usage:
#   make              — build both opt and ref ELFs
#   make compare      — build, run both in QEMU, diff outputs

TOOLCHAIN = /local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5
PREFIX    = $(TOOLCHAIN)/bin/riscv32-elf-

CC  = $(PREFIX)gcc
AS  = $(PREFIX)gcc
LD  = $(PREFIX)gcc

ARCH    = -march=rv32im -mabi=ilp32
CFLAGS  = $(ARCH) -O2 -mvh -Wall
ASFLAGS = $(ARCH)
LDFLAGS = $(ARCH) -mvh -lm

QEMU = /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
QEMU_FLAGS = -cpu andes-n25 -nographic -M andes_ae350 -semihosting -bios

# Assembly sources for sinf and its dependencies
ASM_SRCS = sf_sin.s andes_fpreduct.s andes_fpsincof.s andes_fpcoscof.s
ASM_OBJS = $(ASM_SRCS:.s=.o)

TEST_SRC = test_sinf.c
TEST_OBJ = test_sinf.o

OPT_ELF = test_sinf_opt.elf
REF_ELF = test_sinf_ref.elf

all: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)

# Optimized: link with Andes assembly (objects before -lm to override)
$(OPT_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(ASM_OBJS) $(LDFLAGS)

# Reference: link with toolchain's libm only (no assembly)
$(REF_ELF): $(TEST_OBJ)
	$(LD) -o $@ $(TEST_OBJ) $(LDFLAGS)

$(TEST_OBJ): $(TEST_SRC)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

%.o: %.s
	$(AS) $(ASFLAGS) -c -o $@ $<

compare: $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF)
	@echo "=== Running reference (toolchain libm) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(REF_ELF) > ref_output.txt
	@echo "=== Running optimized (Andes assembly) ==="
	$(QEMU) $(QEMU_FLAGS) $(OPT_ELF) > opt_output.txt
	@echo "=== Diff (< = ref, > = opt) ==="
	@diff ref_output.txt opt_output.txt > diff_output.txt && echo "IDENTICAL" || \
		(echo "$$(wc -l < diff_output.txt) diff lines — see diff_output.txt"; head -40 diff_output.txt)

clean:
	rm -f $(ASM_OBJS) $(TEST_OBJ) $(OPT_ELF) $(REF_ELF) \
	      ref_output.txt opt_output.txt diff_output.txt

.PHONY: all compare clean

I have a few minor improvements to suggest:

1. The QEMU example in "Standalone Test Builds" section shows two different QEMU invocation styles (one with `-cpu rv32 -M virt`, another with `-cpu andes-n25 -M andes_ae350`). The Makefile uses the Andes machine, so the first example could be confusing.

2. The `make compare` target in the Makefile is useful but not documented in the CLAUDE.md.

3. The base `srcs_libm_machine` list (copysign, finite, fma, isinf, isnan, llrint, llround, etc.) is always compiled regardless of config — this isn't mentioned.

Let me apply these small improvements:

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## What This Directory Is

RISC-V architecture-specific math library (`libm`) optimizations for picolibc. These replace generic C implementations from `libm/common/` and `libm/math/` with hand-tuned assembly or hardware-FPU-aware C code.

See `README.md` in this directory for detailed function listings and build configuration matrix.

## Build System

This directory is built as part of the top-level picolibc Meson build. There is no standalone build. The `meson.build` here populates `srcs_libm_machine` which gets compiled into the libm static library.

A separate `CMakeLists.txt` exists for CMake-based builds but only includes a minimal subset (C files, no assembly, no configuration branching).

### Key build flags (set in top-level `meson.build`, consumed here)

- `is_softfp` — software floating-point ABI (no hardware FPU)
- `is_double_abi` — hardware double-precision FPU
- `is_riscv64` — 64-bit RISC-V
- `is_rvm` — M-extension (hardware multiply/divide)
- `is_rv32e` — RV32E reduced 16-register ISA

### Per-target library quirk

For soft-float RV32 configs (both standard and RV32E), `sf_sincos.c` is compiled as a separate per-target `static_library` via the `foreach params : targets` loop, not added to `srcs_libm_machine`. This is because it needs per-multilib `c_args`. The resulting `lib_machine<target>` libraries are linked via `link_whole` elsewhere.

## Assembly Conventions

The `.s` files were originally written for the Andes NDS32 ISA and transliterated to RISC-V. Comments preserve the original NDS32 mnemonics (e.g., `# push $lp`, `# swi $r0, [$sp + #4]`).

### Register mapping pattern

Assembly uses standard RISC-V register names (`x10`–`x20`, `sp`, `ra`, `t6`). The NDS32-to-RISC-V register mapping is:
- `$r0`–`$r5` → `x10`–`x15` (argument/return registers `a0`–`a5`)
- `$r6`–`$r9` → `x9`, `x18`–`x20` (saved registers)
- `$lp` → `ra` (return address)
- `$ta` → `t6` (temporary)

### Internal call graph

Assembly math functions call shared Andes helper routines (`andes_*.s`). Key dependency chains:
- Trig functions → `__gf_dpreduct`/`__gf_fpreduct` (argument reduction) → `__gf_exmul`/`__gf_exadd` (extended precision) → polynomial evaluators (`__gf_p4`–`__gf_p8`)
- `exp`/`log`/`pow` → `__gf_dpexexp`/`__gf_dpexlog` → extended precision helpers
- All assembly shares coefficient tables: `__gf_dpsincon`, `__gf_dpcoscon`, `__gf_fpsincof`, `__gf_fpcoscof`, etc.

### RV32E variants

`rv32e/` contains the same routines rewritten to use only `x0`–`x15` (16 registers). When modifying an assembly routine, the corresponding `rv32e/` version must also be updated.

## Hard-float C Code (`hardfloat/`)

Optimized C implementations that exploit hardware FPU instructions. Originally from ARM's Optimized Routines project (Apache 2.0 licensed).

- Uses `#pragma GCC optimize ("O3")` for aggressive optimization
- `ref_rredf.c`/`ref_rredf.h` provides the shared float argument reduction helper (`ref__mathlib_rredf`)
- Polynomial coefficients are stored in global arrays (not `static const`) to control memory layout for performance

## Standalone Test Builds (outside Meson)

For quick accuracy/correctness testing of individual assembly routines, use the standalone `Makefile` in this directory. It compiles test C files and links them against the relevant assembly objects directly.

### Toolchain

Use the Andes `nds32le-elf-mculib-v5` toolchain for RV32 soft-float builds:

```
/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast542/build-toolchain/linux/nds32le-elf-mculib-v5/bin/riscv32-elf-gcc
```

- GCC 14.2.0, bare-metal (`riscv32-elf`)
- Ships with newlib (provides printf, math reference functions, etc.)
- `-mvh` flag enables Andes virtual hosting (semihosting) for I/O via QEMU/GDB
- Target flags: `-march=rv32im -mabi=ilp32` for soft-float M-extension builds

### QEMU

Use the Andes QEMU v5 build for running RV32 test ELFs:

```
/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32
```

- QEMU 9.2.0 (Andes fork, v9.2.0-401-g4581fc99a4)
- Run a semihosting test ELF:
  ```sh
  /local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32 \
      -cpu rv32 -M virt -nographic -bios none \
      -semihosting -kernel test_sinf.elf
  ```

### Example: build and run sinf test

```sh
make clean && make          # builds test_sinf.elf
/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv32 -cpu andes-n25  -nographic   -M andes_ae350  -semihosting -bios test_sinf.elf
```

## Naming Conventions

- `e_` — IEEE error-handling wrapper functions (e.g., `e_sqrt` wraps the core sqrt)
- `s_` — double-precision standard functions
- `sf_` — single-precision (float) standard functions
- `k_` — kernel helpers (internal, e.g., `k_sin`, `k_cos`)
- `er_` — reentrant variants (e.g., `er_lgamma`)
- `andes_` — shared assembly building blocks (not standalone math functions)
